summary: Luu examines how discussions about AI/ML bias reveal a double standard compared to 'classical' software bugs, where people frequently deny that outputs opposite to user intent (like turning Asian faces white in professional photos) constitute bugs at all. He argues that encoding biases into automated systems isn't new to ML—it's been happening throughout computing history—but ML has made these biases more legible to lay people. The fundamental problem is that consumer markets reward velocity over quality, making it structurally difficult to address bias even when technical solutions might exist.
key_points: When generative AI produces output opposite to user intent (e.g., converting Asian faces to white faces for 'professional' photos), commenters deny this is even a bug—a response that wouldn't happen with classical software bugs

Bias in automated systems predates ML and exists throughout computing (compression algorithms favoring English, forms rejecting Vietnamese names, etc.) but ML makes it more visible to the general public

Common proposed solutions like 'hire more diverse teams' have failed for decades because: executives don't prioritize it, there are too many dimensions of diversity, and diverse hiring doesn't automatically cause issues to be prioritized and fixed

Consumer markets structurally favor velocity over quality (see: Volvo's safety advantage becoming a market disadvantage), making it difficult for any quality-focused solution to succeed without regulatory changes

The author genuinely doesn't know if the technical problem is solvable, drawing parallels to both impossible-seeming problems (1000x faster CPUs) and problems wrongly assumed impossible (efficient software testing)
money_quotes: AI apologists insist this is fine when it comes to things like changing someone's race or ethnicity...it would be absurd to argue that it's fine for my tire change appointment to have been converted to an oil change appointment because other companies have schedulers that convert oil change appointments to tire change appointments

My feeling is that the 'natural', as in lowest energy and most straightforward state for institutions and products is that they don't work very well. If someone hasn't previously instilled a culture or instituted processes that foster quality in a particular dimension, quality is likely to be poor

We've encoded biases into automation for as long as we've had automation and the increased scope and scale of automation has been and will continue to increase the scope and scale of automated bias. It's just that now, many uses of ML make these kinds of biases a lot more legible to lay people

Companies have overwhelmingly chosen velocity over quality...consumers overwhelmingly choose the lower cost or more featureful option over the higher quality option. Despite vehicular accidents being one of the leading causes of death for people under 50, paying for safety is such a low priority for consumers that Volvo has become a niche brand

Even if execs and capital were bought into the idea, it still wouldn't work because there are too many dimensions. You're lucky if the organization is capable of seriously prioritizing diversity in two or three dimensions while dropping the ball on hundreds or thousands of other dimensions
tone: analytical and methodical with underlying frustration
key_insight: AI bias isn't a new problem but rather the latest manifestation of how automated systems have always encoded societal biases—the real challenge is that market incentives systematically favor shipping fast over fixing these issues, making proposed solutions based on 'caring more' or 'diverse hiring' structurally doomed to fail without addressing the underlying economic forces.
